result.markdown:
# Python Reference for Time Series and Sequence Learning This appendix collects the Python patterns used throughout the book. It is designed as a practical companion for the labs. The goal is not to replace documentation for `numpy`, `pandas`, `statsmodels`, `scikit-learn`, `torch`, or `plotly`, but to give students a consistent workflow for simulation, visualization, modeling, validation, and debugging. The guiding principle is simple: in time series, code must respect time order. Random shuffling, scaling with future data, or using future observations as features can make a model look much better than it really is. ## A.1 Recommended Python environment The labs are written for Python 3.10 or later. A standard environment should include the following packages. ::: {#02a44500 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Core scientific stack import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Time series tools from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import acf, pacf, adfuller from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox # Machine learning tools from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression, Ridge from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler # Optional deep learning tools import torch import torch.nn as nn from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader ``` ::: For Google Colab, a minimal setup cell is: ::: {#8a22d01a .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} !pip install numpy pandas matplotlib statsmodels scikit-learn plotly torch --quiet ``` ::: Some optional packages used in advanced labs may require installation only when needed: ::: {#099551a2 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} !pip install prophet pmdarima PyWavelets hmmlearn --quiet ``` ::: ## A.2 Reproducibility Use a fixed seed whenever simulation or randomized training is involved. ::: {#b3ae79fc .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import torch SEED = 7339 rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED) torch.manual_seed(SEED) torch.set_num_threads(1) ``` ::: In time series experiments, reproducibility means more than a seed. It also means recording the forecasting horizon, train/test split date, scaling procedure, feature construction rule, and validation design. ## A.3 Basic time-series objects A univariate time series can be stored as a `pandas.Series` with an integer index or a datetime index. ::: {#d7a4a72e .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} n = 120 dates = pd.date_range("2026-01-01", periods=n, freq="D") y = pd.Series(rng.normal(size=n).cumsum(), index=dates, name="y") y.head() ``` ::: A multivariate time series is usually stored as a `pandas.DataFrame`. ::: {#4e880976 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} X = pd.DataFrame({ "series_a": rng.normal(size=n).cumsum(), "series_b": rng.normal(size=n).cumsum(), "series_c": rng.normal(size=n).cumsum() }, index=dates) X.head() ``` ::: When working with dates, always check the frequency. ::: {#3476cf9e .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(y.index.freq) print(pd.infer_freq(y.index)) ``` ::: ## A.4 Plotting conventions For static plots in Python chunks, use `matplotlib`. For book interactives, Plotly is loaded globally through `assets/header.html`; do not add another Plotly script tag inside a chapter. ::: {#9966fb9f .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3)) y.plot(ax=ax) ax.set_title("Time Series Plot") ax.set_xlabel("time") ax.set_ylabel("value") plt.show() ``` ::: ACF and PACF plots are frequently used for model identification. ::: {#9278f45a .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) plot_acf(y.dropna(), lags=30, ax=ax) ax.set_title("Sample ACF") plt.show() fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) plot_pacf(y.dropna(), lags=30, ax=ax, method="ywm") ax.set_title("Sample PACF") plt.show() ``` ::: ## A.5 Common simulation patterns ### White noise White noise has mean zero, constant variance, and zero autocovariance at nonzero lags. ::: {#8d7a79c6 .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} n = 400 sigma = 1.0 w = rng.normal(0, sigma, size=n) ``` ::: ### Random walk The random walk satisfies $X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t$. ::: {#f969cdb5 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} x_rw = np.cumsum(w) ``` ::: ### AR(1) The AR(1) model is $$ X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + W_t. $$ ::: {#2619452c .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} n = 400 burnin = 200 total = n + burnin phi = 0.75 w = rng.normal(0, 1, size=total) x_full = np.zeros(total) for t in range(1, total): x_full[t] = phi * x_full[t - 1] + w[t] x_ar1 = x_full[burnin:] assert len(x_ar1) == n ``` ::: ### MA(1) The MA(1) model is $$ X_t = W_t + \theta W_{t-1}. $$ ::: {#efe4f322 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} theta = 0.70 x_full = np.zeros(total) for t in range(1, total): x_full[t] = w[t] + theta * w[t - 1] x_ma1 = x_full[burnin:] assert len(x_ma1) == n ``` ::: ### ARMA(1,1) The ARMA(1,1) model is $$ X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + W_t + \theta W_{t-1}. $$ ::: {#20872df8 .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} phi = 0.65 theta = 0.50 x_full = np.zeros(total) for t in range(1, total): x_full[t] = phi * x_full[t - 1] + w[t] + theta * w[t - 1] x_arma11 = x_full[burnin:] assert len(x_arma11) == n ``` ::: ## A.6 Sample autocovariance and ACF from scratch The sample autocovariance at lag $h$ is often estimated by $$ \widehat{\gamma}(h) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=h+1}^{n} (x_t-\bar{x})(x_{t-h}-\bar{x}). $$ ::: {#dcf59c64 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} def sample_autocovariance(x, max_lag): x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float) n = len(x) x_centered = x - x.mean() gamma = [] for h in range(max_lag + 1): value = np.sum(x_centered[h:] * x_centered[:n - h]) / n gamma.append(value) return np.array(gamma) def sample_acf(x, max_lag): gamma = sample_autocovariance(x, max_lag) return gamma / gamma[0] rho_hat = sample_acf(x_ar1, max_lag=20) ``` ::: ## A.7 Train, validation, and test splits For forecasting, split by time order. ::: {#15e771bf .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} def chronological_split(y, train_frac=0.7, val_frac=0.15): n = len(y) n_train = int(train_frac * n) n_val = int(val_frac * n) train = y.iloc[:n_train] val = y.iloc[n_train:n_train + n_val] test = y.iloc[n_train + n_val:] return train, val, test train, val, test = chronological_split(y) ``` ::: Avoid this for forecasting: ::: {#187921d9 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Do not use this for time-series forecasting validation. # from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split # train, test = train_test_split(y, test_size=0.2, shuffle=True) ``` ::: ## A.8 Forecasting metrics For observations $y_t$ and forecasts $\widehat{y}_t$, common point-forecast metrics include $$ \operatorname{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}|y_t-\widehat{y}_t|, $$ $$ \operatorname{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(y_t-\widehat{y}_t)^2}. $$ ::: {#b483934e .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} def rmse(y_true, y_pred): y_true = np.asarray(y_true) y_pred = np.asarray(y_pred) return np.sqrt(np.mean((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)) def mae(y_true, y_pred): y_true = np.asarray(y_true) y_pred = np.asarray(y_pred) return np.mean(np.abs(y_true - y_pred)) ``` ::: For seasonal data, a seasonal naive model is an essential baseline. ::: {#31feb568 .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} def seasonal_naive_forecast(y_train, horizon, season_length): y_train = np.asarray(y_train) if len(y_train) < season_length: raise ValueError("Training series must be at least one seasonal period long.") repeats = int(np.ceil(horizon / season_length)) forecast = np.tile(y_train[-season_length:], repeats)[:horizon] return forecast ``` ::: ## A.9 Rolling-origin backtesting Rolling-origin evaluation repeatedly refits or updates the model using only past data. ::: {#31fef07b .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} def rolling_origin_splits(n, initial_window, horizon, step=1): starts = [] train_ends = [] test_ranges = [] train_end = initial_window while train_end + horizon <= n: starts.append(0) train_ends.append(train_end) test_ranges.append((train_end, train_end + horizon)) train_end += step return list(zip(starts, train_ends, test_ranges)) splits = rolling_origin_splits(n=200, initial_window=100, horizon=12, step=12) ``` ::: A template for evaluating a simple forecasting function is: ::: {#ce749a19 .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} def evaluate_rolling_origin(y, forecast_function, initial_window, horizon, step=1): y = pd.Series(y).reset_index(drop=True) records = [] for start, train_end, (test_start, test_end) in rolling_origin_splits( len(y), initial_window, horizon, step ): y_train = y.iloc[start:train_end] y_test = y.iloc[test_start:test_end] y_pred = forecast_function(y_train, horizon) records.append({ "train_end": train_end, "horizon": horizon, "mae": mae(y_test, y_pred), "rmse": rmse(y_test, y_pred) }) return pd.DataFrame(records) ``` ::: ## A.10 Fitting ARIMA and SARIMA models A basic ARIMA fit uses `statsmodels`. ::: {#c9547d8c .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} model = ARIMA(train, order=(1, 1, 1)) result = model.fit() print(result.summary()) forecast_result = result.get_forecast(steps=len(test)) forecast_mean = forecast_result.predicted_mean forecast_interval = forecast_result.conf_int() ``` ::: A SARIMA model can be fitted with `SARIMAX`. ::: {#09a1d845 .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} model = SARIMAX( train, order=(1, 1, 1), seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 12), enforce_stationarity=False, enforce_invertibility=False ) result = model.fit(disp=False) forecast = result.get_forecast(steps=len(test)) ``` ::: Residual diagnostics should always be checked. ::: {#91d11ba0 .cell execution_count=24} ``` {.python .cell-code} resid = result.resid.dropna() fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) plot_acf(resid, lags=30, ax=ax) ax.set_title("Residual ACF") plt.show() lb = acorr_ljungbox(resid, lags=[10, 20], return_df=True) lb ``` ::: ## A.11 Supervised learning matrix from lags Many machine learning models require turning a time series into a supervised learning table. ::: {#fae65274 .cell execution_count=25} ``` {.python .cell-code} def make_lagged_frame(y, lags, horizon=1): y = pd.Series(y).reset_index(drop=True) data = pd.DataFrame({"y": y}) for lag in lags: data[f"lag_{lag}"] = data["y"].shift(lag) data["target"] = data["y"].shift(-horizon) data = data.dropna().reset_index(drop=True) X = data[[f"lag_{lag}" for lag in lags]] target = data["target"] return X, target X_lag, y_target = make_lagged_frame(y, lags=[1, 2, 3, 7], horizon=1) ``` ::: The scaling rule is important. Fit scalers only on training data. ::: {#6e1608f8 .cell execution_count=26} ``` {.python .cell-code} split = int(0.8 * len(X_lag)) X_train, X_test = X_lag.iloc[:split], X_lag.iloc[split:] y_train, y_test = y_target.iloc[:split], y_target.iloc[split:] scaler = StandardScaler() X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train) X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test) model = Ridge(alpha=1.0) model.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train) y_pred = model.predict(X_test_scaled) print("MAE:", mae(y_test, y_pred)) print("RMSE:", rmse(y_test, y_pred)) ``` ::: ## A.12 Window datasets for neural networks For neural networks, a common supervised learning format is $$ (x_{t-L+1},\ldots,x_t) \mapsto (x_{t+1},\ldots,x_{t+H}). $$ ::: {#e065cace .cell execution_count=27} ``` {.python .cell-code} def make_windows(y, input_length, horizon): y = np.asarray(y, dtype=np.float32) X = [] Y = [] for start in range(0, len(y) - input_length - horizon + 1): end = start + input_length X.append(y[start:end]) Y.append(y[end:end + horizon]) return np.array(X), np.array(Y) X_win, Y_win = make_windows(y.values, input_length=24, horizon=12) print(X_win.shape, Y_win.shape) ``` ::: A minimal PyTorch dataset is: ::: {#3e594019 .cell execution_count=28} ``` {.python .cell-code} class WindowDataset(Dataset): def __init__(self, X, Y): self.X = torch.tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32) self.Y = torch.tensor(Y, dtype=torch.float32) def __len__(self): return len(self.X) def __getitem__(self, idx): return self.X[idx], self.Y[idx] train_ds = WindowDataset(X_win, Y_win) train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=32, shuffle=False) ``` ::: For forecasting, avoid shuffling validation and test windows. Shuffling training windows is sometimes acceptable for neural-network optimization after the windows have been created without leakage, but validation must remain chronological. ## A.13 Minimal neural network forecaster ::: {#29e7fc84 .cell execution_count=29} ``` {.python .cell-code} class MLPForecaster(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_length, horizon, hidden=64): super().__init__() self.net = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(input_length, hidden), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(hidden, horizon) ) def forward(self, x): return self.net(x) model = MLPForecaster(input_length=24, horizon=12) optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3) loss_fn = nn.MSELoss() ``` ::: A small training loop: ::: {#ea49bc77 .cell execution_count=30} ``` {.python .cell-code} for epoch in range(20): model.train() total_loss = 0.0 for xb, yb in train_loader: optimizer.zero_grad() pred = model(xb) loss = loss_fn(pred, yb) loss.backward() optimizer.step() total_loss += loss.item() * len(xb) avg_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader.dataset) print(f"epoch={epoch + 1:02d}, train_mse={avg_loss:.4f}") ``` ::: ## A.14 Causal convolution helper A 1D causal convolution should not use future values. The output at time $t$ may depend on $x_t,x_{t-1},x_{t-2},\ldots$, but not on $x_{t+1}$. ::: {#bc233925 .cell execution_count=31} ``` {.python .cell-code} def causal_convolution_1d(x, kernel, dilation=1): x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float) kernel = np.asarray(kernel, dtype=float) n = len(x) k = len(kernel) y = np.zeros(n) for t in range(n): value = 0.0 for j in range(k): idx = t - dilation * j if idx >= 0: value += kernel[j] * x[idx] y[t] = value return y ``` ::: The receptive field of a stack of dilated convolutions with kernel size $K$ and dilations $d_1,\ldots,d_L$ is $$ R = 1 + (K-1)\sum_{\ell=1}^{L} d_\ell. $$ ## A.15 Attention helper functions The scaled dot-product attention formula is $$ \operatorname{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \operatorname{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V. $$ ::: {#cad10cd1 .cell execution_count=32} ``` {.python .cell-code} def softmax_rows(A): A = A - A.max(axis=1, keepdims=True) E = np.exp(A) return E / E.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None): d_k = Q.shape[-1] scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k) if mask is not None: scores = scores + mask weights = softmax_rows(scores) output = weights @ V return output, weights def causal_mask(T): M = np.triu(np.ones((T, T)), k=1) return np.where(M == 1, -1e9, 0.0) ``` ::: ## A.16 Plotly interactive pattern for this book Plotly.js is included once globally in `assets/header.html`. Interactive modules should use the shared configuration in `assets/ts-interactives.js`. A standard pattern is: ```{js} //| eval: false window.TSInteractives = window.TSInteractives || {}; window.TSInteractives.exampleModule = function () { const plotDiv = document.getElementById("example-plot"); if (!plotDiv || typeof Plotly === "undefined") return; const traces = [ { x: [0, 1, 2], y: [0, 1, 0], mode: "lines", name: "example" } ]; const layout = { title: "Example Interactive", margin: { t: 60, r: 30, b: 50, l: 50 } }; Plotly.react( plotDiv, traces, layout, window.TSInteractives.plotlyConfig ); }; document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () { window.TSInteractives.exampleModule(); }); ``` The chapter `.qmd` file should contain only the container, not another Plotly script tag. ```{markdown} ```html

Interactive Module

``` ``` ## A.17 Common debugging checklist When a lab or interactive does not work, check these items first. - Confirm the notebook runtime is using Python 3.10 or later. - Run all cells from the top instead of running isolated cells out of order. - Check array lengths after differencing, lagging, and windowing. - Use `assert` statements for expected shapes. - Make sure scaling is fitted only on the training set. - Make sure validation and test sets are later in time than the training set. - Check that `assets/header.html` loads Plotly.js once. - Check browser developer tools for missing file errors such as `404 Not Found`. - Check that each interactive container ID matches the JavaScript code. - Avoid raw HTML tables inside Quarto documents when rendering to PDF. Useful shape checks: ::: {#8964cba7 .cell execution_count=33} ``` {.python .cell-code} assert len(y_train) > 0 assert len(y_test) > 0 assert X_train.shape[0] == len(y_train) assert X_test.shape[0] == len(y_test) assert np.isfinite(X_train).all().all() assert np.isfinite(y_train).all() ``` ::: ## A.18 AI-assisted Python workflow AI tools are helpful for code review, but they must be asked precise, time-series-aware questions. Good prompts include the data structure, the forecast horizon, the information set, and the validation design. Example prompt: > I am forecasting $y_{t+12}$ using only information available up to time $t$. Here is my feature construction and train/test split. Check whether any feature leaks future information. Also check whether my scaling is fitted only on the training set. Another useful prompt: > Here is my residual ACF, Ljung-Box result, and rolling-origin error table. Give three possible reasons the model fails, and propose checks that I can verify with code. AI-generated code should be treated as a draft. Before using it in a project, verify the following. - The model does not use future observations as predictors. - Differencing, scaling, and feature engineering are performed in the correct chronological order. - The validation method matches the forecasting task. - Baselines are included. - Residual diagnostics are checked. - The code is reproducible from a fresh runtime. ## A.19 Minimal project notebook structure A strong final-project notebook should follow this structure. 1. Problem statement and forecast contract. 2. Data loading and cleaning. 3. Exploratory time-series analysis. 4. Baseline models. 5. Main models. 6. Rolling-origin or chronological validation. 7. Residual diagnostics and uncertainty. 8. Interpretation and limitations. 9. Final conclusion. A simple project header can be: ::: {#15ba01e7 .cell execution_count=34} ``` {.python .cell-code} PROJECT_TITLE = "Short descriptive title" FORECAST_TARGET = "variable to forecast" FORECAST_HORIZON = 12 INFORMATION_SET = "all variables observed up to forecast origin t" VALIDATION_SCHEME = "rolling-origin evaluation" RANDOM_SEED = 7339 ``` ::: ## A.20 Key principle For sequence data, the order is part of the data. A model that ignores the order may still be useful, but a validation procedure that ignores the order is usually misleading.
